coredata.dc:title: Interactions between europium and terbium ions and products of starch thermal decomposition: Spectroscopic and quantum chemical study
coredata.dc:creator: Dzharlagasova, D.N.
Khokhlova, A.R.
Zakharova, T.V.
Pozharov, M.V.
coredata.dc:description: Addition of lanthanide ions to carbon nanoparticles produced from biological polymers such as starch may significantly improve luminescent characteristics of such nanoparticles. We have synthesized carbon nanoparticles from starch in the presence of europium and terbium ions and studied their dimensions and optical properties. We also did a quantum chemical calculation of possible compounds formed in a solution obtained after hydrothermal treatment of starch in the presence of europium and terbium ions. After 3 h of alkaline hydrolysis of starch at a temperature of 160 °C in the autoclave in the presence of Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions, we observed the formation of nanoparticles with varying size that have luminescent properties different from those of initial substances. Quantum chemical calculations of potential products show that a Eu complex with starch residues (1:5 ratio) is the most thermodynamically possible, and the Eu complex with levoglucosan is the least possible. The probability of europium and terbuim complexation with potential hydrocarbon ligands increases with the appearance of additional bonds that impart stability to the compounds. Of the compounds studied, the most effective donors are complexes with starch units, with the donor properties weakening as the number of links increases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide complexes
Carbon dots
Polysaccharides
Luminescence
Semi-empirical calculations

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DLS analysis of solutions containing carbon dots
Results of quantum chemical calculations of the studied structures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Particle size distribution in studied solutions (green – Tb, red – Eu). (Color online.)
Zeta potential distribution for studied solutions (green – Tb, red – Eu). (Color online.)
Emission spectra of carbon dots compared to initial starch solution.
Emission spectra of solutions containing products of thermal treatment of starch in the presence of Tb (a) and Eu chlorides (b).
Influence of lanthanide ions on luminescence intensity of carbon dot solutions (exitation source wavelength – 418 nm).
Structure of studied europium compounds with fructose (a), levoglucosan (b), glucose (c) and starch fragments (d).
Maximum absorption bands in the calculated spectra.

head.ce:title: Interactions between europium and terbium ions and products of starch thermal decomposition: Spectroscopic and quantum chemical study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Saratov State University, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have been synthesized from starch solutions containing Eu3+ (red line) and Tb3+ (green line) ions. Synthesized carbon dots vary in size and surface potential and have luminescent properties different from initial solutions. Semiempirical calculations of potential complex structures show that the most thermodynamically probable is the formation of Eu complex with starch at 5 to 1 ligand to metal ratio.

Addition of lanthanide ions to carbon nanoparticles produced from biological polymers such as starch may significantly improve luminescent characteristics of such nanoparticles. We have synthesized carbon nanoparticles from starch in the presence of europium and terbium ions and studied their dimensions and optical properties. We also did a quantum chemical calculation of possible compounds formed in a solution obtained after hydrothermal treatment of starch in the presence of europium and terbium ions. After 3 h of alkaline hydrolysis of starch at a temperature of 160 °C in the autoclave in the presence of Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions, we observed the formation of nanoparticles with varying size that have luminescent properties different from those of initial substances. Quantum chemical calculations of potential products show that a Eu complex with starch residues (1:5 ratio) is the most thermodynamically possible, and the Eu complex with levoglucosan is the least possible. The probability of europium and terbuim complexation with potential hydrocarbon ligands increases with the appearance of additional bonds that impart stability to the compounds. Of the compounds studied, the most effective donors are complexes with starch units, with the donor properties weakening as the number of links increases.

ce:section.ce:para: Complex compounds of lanthanides are considered promising materials for new optoelectronic devices such as organic light-emitting diodes, optical amplifiers, lasers, flexible polychrome displays, etc. [1–3]. Phosphors with red (EuIII), green (TbIII) and blue (EuII) color of luminescence, as well as their combinations, effectively convert ultraviolet radiation into visible light. These materials find widespread application as cathode-ray tubes, plasma panels and energy-saving fluorescent lamps [4]. The possibility of sensitizing the luminescence of trivalent europium with organic aromatic molecules makes its complexes useful for such highly sensitive applications as materials for luminescent labels [5]. Since the early 1980s, nanoparticles of europium complexes have been widely used as labels for assays that use time-resolved luminescence. Europium with its long lifetime of the excited state is an excellent marker that allows its detectable luminescence to be isolated, cutting off the background “fast” luminescence of fluorescent compounds present in living cells and tissues [6]. In addition, since the absorption of light by tissues decreases with increasing wavelength, even low-intensive red light emitted by europium is sufficient for it to be detectable. Luminescent bioassays based on europium compounds are now widely used in life sciences, including for bio-imaging.

Ln (III) complexes in optoelectronic devices are most often used in the form of composites with conjugated polymers. The process of light absorption by such composites consists of the following stages. The polymer, having absorbed a photon, passes into an excited state and transfers energy to excited levels of the ligand. The ligand serving as an “antenna” goes over into a singlet excited state, then due to rapid intercombination conversion into a lower triplet one. Then, the excitation energy is transferred from the triplet level of the ligand to the resonance energy level of the Ln (III) ion, for example, at the 5D0 and 5D1 sublevels of the Eu (III) multiplet and 5D4 of the Tb (III) ion. The excited ion can either give back energy or go to the ground state, emitting a quantum of light in the visible region of the spectrum [7]. The luminescence efficiency of composites containing Ln (III) ions is determined by the degree of overlapping between emission spectrum of the polymer and absorption spectrum of the complex.

Complexes of lanthanides (in particular, Eu and Tb) with organic ligands (eg. alginates) are widely used in medicine as biolabels for determining the presence of various pathogenic organisms, and starch complexes with Eu3+ are used as pharmaceutical transportation agents [8]. On the other hand, starch can act as a starting material for the production of carbon quantum dots [9]. The main advantage of carbon quantum dots is the high intensity of their luminescence with a wide excitation spectrum. The addition of lanthanide ions to carbon dots significantly changes the spectral characteristics - for example, it allows increasing the intensity and lifetime of their luminescence, which is important for the medical probes development [10–12].

The possibility of a theoretical study of Ln (III) complexes with the largest overlap of the absorption spectrum with the emission spectrum of polymers allows for targeted synthesis of highly effective luminescent materials with promising optical and operational properties. In this connection, the applicability of the Sparkle model for lanthanide complexes, specially developed for MOPAC software, where the Ln (III) ion is represented as a point charge placed in the center of the repulsive spherical potential, is relevant [13].

Thus, for the synthesis and subsequent study of luminescent properties, the readily available polysaccharide (starch) was selected as the organic fragment, and Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions that have luminescent bands in the visible light region were used as lanthanide ions. The paper also presents the results of semiempirical calculations of assumed products of complexation between saccharides and products of their thermal decomposition and lanthanide ions.
The synthesis of carbon dots from starch in the presence of lanthanide ions was carried out according to the following procedure:

2 g of starch and 0.2 g of NaOH were dissolved in 30 ml of bidistilled water. The solution was split into two glass cylinders. 15 ml of 10−4 M EuCl3 solution was added to one cylinder and 15 ml of 10−4 M TbCl3 solution was added to the other. All reagents used were of analytical grade quality.

The flasks were placed into autoclaves, sealed tightly to exclude the possibility of water evaporation and heated for 4 h at a temperature of 160 °C. As a result, two brown solutions were obtained and centrifuged separately at a speed of 7500 rpm for 5 min. After this, the resulting solutions were diluted 1000 times to reduce the color saturation and investigated via diffraction light scattering method using Zetasizer Nano DS (manufactured by Malvern Instruments).
Luminescent properties of obtained solutions were studied via Cary Eclipse fluorescent spectrometer (manufacturer – Agilent Technologies).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DLS analysis of solutions containing carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The diagram of the size distribution in relation to the intensity of the signal is shown in Fig. 1 .

As can be seen from Fig. 1, in the solution obtained by alkaline hydrolysis of starch in the presence of EuCl3 (Fig. 1, red), two types of particles are formed with the predominance of particles sized 210 ± 61 nm (contribution – 98%). Larger particles (5.4 ± 0.3 µm in diameter) are present in smaller amount (contribution – 1.5%). For a sample containing Tb and starch hydrolysis products (Fig. 1, green), three types of particles were found – the smallest particles were sized 3.3 ± 0.6 nm, the greatest contribution to DLS spectra was made by particles sized 247 ± 47 nm (84%) while the largest particles (∼5500 nm) provide 10% of contribution to DLS signal.

The sign of the surface charge of the obtained particles can be estimated with the help of the magnitude of the zeta potential (the potential formed when the surface of sliding of the colloidal particles moves). The results of determining the zeta potential are shown in Fig. 2 .

As can be seen from the figure, both solutions produce a negative zeta potential (−24 ± 6 mV in the case of Eu3+ solution and 33 ± 9 mV in the case of Tb3+ solution) is observed. The presence of a strong negative charge in particles containing lanthanide ions may indicate an insufficient concentration of lanthanide ions.

In order to show the impact of Eu3+ and Tb3+ addition, we have compared emission spectra of synthezed carbon dot solutions with emission spectra of starch solution treated in autoclave without addition of lanthanide chlorides.
The emission spectra of the products of thermal treatment of starch in the presence of NaOH are shown in Fig. 3 . Numbers in the legend refer to the wavelength of the excitation source.

As we can see from the Figure, thermal treatment leads to the destruction of glycosidic bonds in the starch molecule with the subsequent formation of compounds with emission spectrum shifting together with the wavelength of the excitation source – a pattern characteristic for carbon dots. The addition of Tb3+ (Fig. 4 a) and Eu3+ (Fig. 4b) ions to initial starch solutions leads to a decrease in the total luminescence intensity of the carbon dots, with a bathochromic shift of the emission band maxima observed.

It should also be noted that the higher is the intensity of emission band in the initial solution of carbon dots (Fig. 4), the smaller is the intensity of corresponding band in solutions containing lanthanide ions. This may indicate the coordination of the products of alkaline hydrolysis of starch around metal ions with subsequent transfer of excitation energy to the metal. At the same time, characteristic bands of europium (590, 612 nm) and terbium (480, 545 nm) ions are not observed, possibly due to a rather high concentration products of hydrolysis and self-absorption of solutions.

If we compare the intensities of the corresponding bands (for example, at an excitation source wavelength of 418 nm) in the spectra of alkaline starch products before and after the addition of lanthanide ions, we can note that, in coordination with Eu, the band intensity is almost 2 times higher than in the case of Tb (Fig. 5 ). This may be caused by formation of more stable bonds between the hydrolysis products and the metal atoms, and also because the selected wavelength of the excitation source is greater than the excitation channels of the Eu3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of quantum chemical calculations of the studied structures
ce:section.ce:para: The structures of potential products of lanthanide ion interactions with products of starch decompositions were calculated using MOPAC 2016 software [14] via semi-empirical method PM6/SPARKLE [13].

The objects of the study were Eu3+ compounds with polysaccharide units with varying number of monomers (st), as well as monomer products of starch hydrolysis – glucose (glu), fructose (fru) and levoglucosan (lgz). The potential ligands were chosen according to literary data [15,16]. The structure of the investigated compounds is shown in Fig. 6 .
Calculated parameters of studied compounds are provided in Table 1 .

According to Table 1, the formation of all calculated structures in solution is possible, with the formation of a Eu(st)5 complex being the most probable. The possibility of europium bonding with starch residues can be explained by the fact that starch itself can incorporate guest molecules into its structure, thereby creating additional intramolecular hydrogen bonds that impart strength to these complexes. The formation of a complex with the individual fructose molecule Eu (fru) (−2.72 MJ/mol) is less likely due to the smaller number of intramolecular bonds that impart additional stability to the complex.

Of the compounds studied, the most effective donors are complexes with starch units, with the donor properties weakening with the increase in the number of bonds. Complexes with individual molecules of glucose, fructose and levoglucosan are inactive as the energies of the boundary orbitals are located substantially below the zero level.

The difference between EHOMO and ELUMO (ΔE) determines the chemical activity (donor-acceptor properties) and the optical properties of the molecule. The structure of Eu(st)3 with the lowest value of the ionization potential (4.81 eV) will be more active, and the compound Eu(fru) (8.30 eV) exhibits the lowest activity. This can be explained by the low probability of the formation of the structure of Eu (fru), and therefore its participation in reactions by the donor-acceptor mechanism in solution is also unlikely. It can also be noted that Eu(st)3 has a higher absorbing power, since the smaller the chemical potential (ΔE), the greater the wavelength of the absorbed light (the path necessary to spend for the transition of electrons from the lower sublevel to the higher one) and the greater the intensity absorption bands.

For the compounds studied by us, the absorption spectra were calculated using the semi-empirical ZINDO/S method [17,18] implemented in the Orca v.2.4.1 software complex [19]. In this method, the lanthanide atom does not participate directly in the quantum chemical calculations, but is replaced by a +3 point charge, after which a multiconfiguration analysis of the electronic transitions is performed during excitation of the ground state (which was used previously for the optimized geometry of the Eu complexes).

As we can see from Table 2 , the spectrum of Eu(st)3 complex has several low-intensity bands in the range from 180 to 250 nm peaking at 213 nm. These bands most likely correspond to n-σ * and π-σ * electron transitions in the organic ligand. The spectrum of Eu(st)4 complex has two fairly narrow bands in the range from 180 to 350 nm with absorption maxima at 227 and 324 nm. The first band corresponds to π-π * and the second corresponds to n-π * electron transitions in ligand molecules.

In contrast to Eu(st)3 and Eu(st)4 compounds, the absorption spectrum of Eu(st)5 contains only a single very wide band with a maximum at 180 nm (Table 2). This band is close to the characteristic energy transfer in the Eu3+ ion (53190 cm−1, 188 nm [20]), therefore it is possible that an effective transfer of the excitation energy from the ligand to the metal can also be observed.

There are several bands in the spectrum of compound of composition Eu (glu), of which the most intense are bands with maxima at 206 (58%) and 226 nm (68%) (Table 2). At the same time, a slightly less intense band with a maximum at 186 nm (37%) can promote the transfer of energy to the Eu3+ ion, because it is close to the characteristic transition of 4f-5d electrons in the Eu3+ ion (188 nm).

The spectrum of Eu(glu)3 compound is similar to Eu(st)4 as it has broad bands in the range from 180 to 600 nm with maxima at 219 (more intense), 327 and 339 nm (less intense). The first band corresponds to π-π * transitions, and the remaining band corresponds to n-π * transitions in ligand molecules.

The spectrum of Eu(glu)5 compound has several low-intensity bands in the range 200–400 nm are observed, of which the most intense band with a maximum at 210 nm corresponding to π-π * transitions. As the absorption intensity for this complex is sufficiently low, no characteristic luminescence of the lanthanide ion will be observed.

As for Eu(fru) complex, two main bands are observed in the range from 180 to 250 nm with absorption maxima at 214 and 249 nm, of which the second (43%) is more intense. This band is located far enough from the bands of characteristic transitions in Eu3+ ion, therefore, for Eu(fru) complex, the characteristic luminescence of the lanthanide ions is unlikely.
The spectrum of the Eu (lgz) complex does not contain any bands of significant intensity, which is probably due to the small stability of such a compound.

Thus, it was found that the most thermodynamically probable is the formation of complexes with starch links with 5 to 1 ligand to metal ratio and the least probable is the complex Eu with levoglucosan. The probability of formation of europium complexes with the investigated ligands increases with the appearance of additional bonds that impart stability to the compounds. Of the compounds studied, the most effective donors are complexes with starch units, with the donor properties weakening as the number of links increases. Complexes with individual molecules of glucose, fructose and levoglucosan are inactive as energies of their boundary orbitals are located substantially below zero point.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Russian Science foundation (project 16-13-10195).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Industrial gums, polysaccharides and their derivatives

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: MOPAC2016, James J.P. Stewart, Stewart computational chemistry, Colorado Springs, CO, USA, HTTP://OpenMOPAC.net, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Wiley Interdiscip. Rev.: Comput. Mol. Sci.
